title: Rectangles Day Bed
summary: A twin daybed built entirely from small pocket-hole rectangles — no plywood, no panels, just repeated frames that read as one geometric piece.
description:
This daybed skips the usual box-and-panel construction and instead builds the whole headboard and footboard out of nine identical small rectangle frames, joined into three columns of three. It's a look borrowed straight from window-pane furniture, and the trick to pulling it off is building the frames as a batch — cut every rectangle side, joiner, and cross-piece before you assemble a single one, so all nine come out exactly the same size.

Structurally, the rectangle grid is decorative; the actual bed strength comes from the 2x3 aprons and 2x2 legs it's screwed to. Build the H-shaped leg-and-apron frames first and get those square and solid, then treat the rectangle grid as infill you attach to a structure that's already doing the real work. Pocket holes throughout make this realistic without a router or dado stack — the only real skill is patience with nine identical small assemblies.
timeLabel: 8–12 hrs — most of it is the nine repeated rectangle frames

tools.note: Every joint in this build is a pocket screw — a jig is not optional here.

materials.name: 1x2 board, 8 ft
materials.note: All 63 pieces of the rectangle grid come from this stock — cut every piece before assembling anything.

materials.name: 2x2 board, 8 ft
materials.note: Legs, back top rail, and cleats.

materials.name: 2x3 board, 8 ft
materials.note: The load-bearing aprons — this is what the mattress actually rests on.

materials.name: Mattress slats or bunkie board
materials.note: Cut slats from scrap 1x stock spaced per your mattress maker's guidance, or buy a bunkie board.

steps.title: Cut and pocket-hole every rectangle piece first
steps.body: You are probably wondering why you'd start with the small rectangles instead of the leg frames. Because once a rectangle is boxed in on both sides by its neighbors, you can no longer get a drill in to add pocket holes to it. So batch-cut all 24 sides, 24 tops/bottoms, 9 rectangle joiners, and 6 end joiners now, and drill pocket holes (set for 3/4" stock) into every joiner and top/bottom piece before you assemble a single rectangle.

steps.title: Assemble nine identical rectangle frames
steps.body: Build nine "H"-shaped frames: two side pieces joined by a center joiner, using pocket screws and glue set for 1-1/2" stock. Build all nine to the same reference so they end up genuinely identical — any variance here shows immediately once they're side by side.

steps.title: Join rectangles into columns of three
steps.body: Add the tops and bottoms to each H-frame to close out the rectangle shape, then join three rectangles end to end into a column using the end joiners. Work on a flat, level surface and check each rectangle for square as you close it — small errors compound fast across three joined frames.

steps.title: Build the leg-and-apron frames
steps.body: Build two H-shaped end frames from the 2x2 legs and 2x3 side aprons — these carry the actual load of the bed and mattress, so pocket-screw and glue every joint solidly.

steps.title: Attach the rectangle columns to the leg frames
steps.body: Screw the three rectangle columns to the tops and bottoms of the leg-and-apron frames, closing the daybed into its final rectangular footprint. Confirm square across the whole frame with a diagonal tape measurement before locking everything down.

steps.title: Build and attach the arm assemblies
steps.body: Build two more small frames from the arm tops and arm aprons — these become the raised side arms of the daybed. If you're using pocket holes, mirror the two so the visible screw holes land on the outside faces of both arms, not facing into the bed.

steps.title: Attach cleats and slats
steps.body: Fasten the mattress-support cleats to the inside faces of the side aprons, flush to the bottom, with 2-1/2" screws and glue. Rest slats across them, spaced per your mattress recommendation, or drop in a bunkie board.

steps.title: Sand and finish
steps.body: Sand every rectangle edge and every apron and arm — there are a lot of exposed corners in this design and all of them get touched. If this bed will see everyday use rather than occasional lounging, consider reinforcing the rectangle-column-to-leg-frame joints with extra screws; it's a lighter structure than a solid-panel daybed.

images.alt: Twin daybed built from a grid of small wooden rectangle frames
